attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for review of her type 2 diabetes. She was diagnosed with diabetes eight years ago and has been taking metformin 1000 mg twice daily for the past five years. Four months ago, linagliptin 5 mg daily was added as her glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c) was 7.6% (60 mmol/mol). Her medical history includes hypertension and dyslipidaemia. Her medications include metformin, linagliptin, perindopril, and atorvastatin. She works as an accountant and does not smoke or drink alcohol.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index is 29 kg/m² Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations are unremarkable. Recent investigations reveal HbA1c 7.4% (57 mmol/mol), estimated glomerular filtration rate 75 mL/min/1.73m² (>90), and urine albumin:creatinine ratio 2.5 mg/mmol (<3.5).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Continue current medications and review in three months
Add gliclazide modified release 30 mg daily
Add basal insulin glargine 10 units before bed
Cease linagliptin and commence empagliflozin 10mg daily
attributes.explanation:
This patient has type 2 diabetes that remains suboptimally controlled despite combination therapy with metformin and linagliptin (a dipeptidyl peptidase-4 inhibitor). The key issue is that after four months of linagliptin therapy, her HbA1c has only decreased from 7.6% to 7.4%, representing a reduction of just 0.2%.

Cease linagliptin and commence empagliflozin 10mg daily is the most appropriate next step. According to clinical guidelines, any diabetes medication should be discontinued if it fails to reduce HbA1c by at least 0.5% after 3-6 months of treatment. In this case, linagliptin has produced an inadequate glycaemic response. SGLT2 inhibitors are an appropriate alternative second-line agent with additional benefits including weight loss, blood pressure reduction, and cardiovascular and renal protection. Given this patient's age and risk factors (hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and slightly elevated BMI), an SGLT2 inhibitor would be particularly beneficial.

Continue current medications and review in three months is not appropriate as the patient has already had four months of treatment with linagliptin with inadequate response. Continuing an ineffective medication wastes time and resources and delays achieving glycaemic targets.

Add gliclazide modified release 30 mg daily would mean adding a third agent while continuing an ineffective medication (linagliptin). Additionally, sulfonylureas carry a higher risk of hypoglycaemia and weight gain compared to SGLT2 inhibitors. While they are effective at lowering blood glucose, they do not offer the same cardiovascular and renal benefits as SGLT2 inhibitors.

Add semaglutide 0.25mg weekly would involve adding a third agent (a GLP-1 receptor agonist) while continuing an ineffective medication. While GLP-1 receptor agonists are effective for glycaemic control and offer cardiovascular and renal benefits, it would be more appropriate to first replace the ineffective DPP-4 inhibitor rather than adding another medication. Additionally, adding GLP-1 receptor agonists to DPP-4 inhibitor is inappropriate prescribing due to significant overlap in their mechanism of action without additional clinical benefit and increased risk of side effects.

Add basal insulin glargine 10 units before bed is not appropriate for several reasons. Initiating insulin therapy would be premature in this patient who has only modest hyperglycaemia (HbA1c 7.4%) and has not yet tried other oral agents. Insulin therapy carries risks of hypoglycaemia and weight gain and is generally reserved for patients with more severe hyperglycaemia or those who have failed multiple oral agents. Additionally, adding insulin while continuing an ineffective medication (linagliptin) is not a rational approach to therapy intensification. Basal insulin is typically considered when oral medications have been optimized or when HbA1c is significantly elevated (typically >9%).
learningPoints: Dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPD-4) inhibitors should be discontinued if they fail to reduce HbA1c by at least 0.5% after 3-6 months of treatment.
Sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 (SGLT2) inhibitors are an appropriate second-line agent for type 2 diabetes with additional cardiovascular and renal benefits.
Treatment intensification in type 2 diabetes should be guided by individual patient factors including comorbidities, risk of hypoglycaemia, and medication side effects.
references: The Royal Australian College of General Practitioners. Management of type 2 diabetes: A handbook for general practice. East Melbourne, Vic: RACGP; 2020. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/7fe75f75-56e0-40e6-a433-12bd15537c5a/Management-of-type-2-diabetes-A-handbook-for-general-practice.aspx

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree

attributes.name: Level III
attributes.description: Apply, analyze, evaluate, and create (implementing, organizing, and critiquing information)
